title: Trigeminal Neuralgia
oneLiner: Paroxysmal, severe, unilateral facial pain in CN V distribution; often idiopathic or due to vascular compression; can be debilitating and misdiagnosed as dental pain.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 68-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 6-week history of “electric shock” pains in her right cheek and jaw. She winces when speaking and avoids touching her face. The pain comes in brief bursts lasting seconds, occurring up to 20 times per day. She saw her dentist twice, had a root canal, and even an extraction – but the pain persists. She can’t brush her teeth or shave. Neurological exam is normal.

**Pattern Clues Dissected:**

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Unilateral pain in cheek/jaw | Trigeminal nerve (V2/V3) distribution |
| Electric shock / stabbing quality | Neuralgic pain, not nociceptive |
| Brief paroxysms (seconds) | Characteristic of trigeminal neuralgia (TN) |
| Triggered by light touch, chewing, talking | Abnormally hypersensitive trigeminovascular system |
| Normal neurological exam | No sensory loss / motor weakness (if present, consider mass lesion or multiple sclerosis) |
| Age >50 | Classic idiopathic TN peaks in 50s–70s |
| Multiple dental procedures with no relief | Classic “red flag” – always ask about dental history |
| Avoids face-touching / uses hand to protect | “Trigger zone” phenomenon – patient behaviour gives it away |

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

1. **Pain in the first division (V1) only** – present as retro-orbital or forehead pain. May mimic cluster headache or sinusitis. Clue: pain is electric, not pulsatile, and involves the eye.
2. **Constant dull ache between paroxysms** – seen in refractory TN or when neurovascular conflict is severe. Can be confused with trigeminal neuropathy.
3. **Bilateral pain** – very rare in classic TN; think MS or brainstem lesion. Always examine for other neurological signs.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Trigeminal neuralgia is a disorder of the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V) characterised by sudden, severe, brief, recurrent episodes of electric shock–like pain in the face. It’s like having a short-circuit in the nerve – a harmless touch becomes “loaded” and fires a pain signal.

**Why it matters clinically:**  
- Frequency: ~4–13 per 100,000; more common in women and in the elderly.  
- Stakes: Misdiagnosis leads to unnecessary dental extractions, root canals, or temporomandibular joint (TMJ) surgery.  
- Common pitfalls: Delayed treatment (suffering for months); missing secondary causes like multiple sclerosis (MS) or posterior fossa tumour.  
- Good news: Highly treatable – carbamazepine is effective in 70–80%. Bad news: refractory cases may require neurosurgery.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The Story:**  
The trigeminal nerve exits the brainstem and passes through the cerebellopontine angle. In most cases of trigeminal neuralgia, a blood vessel (usually the superior cerebellar artery) loops and compresses the nerve at the root entry zone. This causes demyelination of the nerve’s myelin sheath – the insulation that normally keeps signals fast and private. Once demyelinated, the nerve becomes hyperexcitable. Neighbouring axons cross-talk (ephaptic transmission) and a single light touch on the face can trigger a massive burst of impulses that the brain interprets as excruciating pain.

**Why each key sign happens:**

| Sign | Pathophys mechanism |
|------|---------------------|
| Electric shock quality | Synchronous high-frequency firing of demyelinated axons |
| Brief paroxysms | After a burst, the nerve is briefly refractory – then ready again |
| Trigger zones (cheek, lip, gum) | Demyelinated areas have lowered threshold – touch is enough to depolarise them |
| Unilateral | Lesion is on one side of the nerve (often vascular loop) |
| Normal motor/sensory exam | Demyelination is in sensory axons; motor function (muscles of mastication) is spared unless the motor root is affected (rare; then think mass lesion) |
| No autonomic symptoms | Unlike cluster headache – no tearing, rhinorrhea, miosis |
| Progressive frequency | Over time, demyelination worsens; ephaptic transmission becomes easier |

**Connection to pattern recognition:** The patient who avoids touching her face is protecting the trigger zones. The dental history reveals misdirected treatment – a key pointer that the pain is neuropathic, not odontogenic.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Cause Category | Example / Specific Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic / Classic** | Neurovascular compression (most common) | Age >50; no other neurological signs; excellent response to anticonvulsants |
| **Secondary – Demyelination** | Multiple sclerosis | Bilateral TN; young adult; prior optic neuritis or other MS symptoms; brainstem signs on exam |
| **Secondary – Mass lesion** | Acoustic neuroma, meningioma, epidermoid cyst | Headache, hearing loss, ataxia, trigeminal sensory loss, cranial neuropathies (e.g., CN VII, VIII) |
| **Secondary – Vascular** | Arteriovenous malformation, aneurysm (rare) | Pulsatile tinnitus; focal deficit; may be younger |
| **Secondary – Inflammatory** | Sarcoidosis, vasculitis | Multisystem involvement (lungs, skin, eyes); raised ACE |
| **Postherpetic neuralgia** | Herpes zoster affecting CN V | Vesicular rash before pain; pain is constant + paroxysmal; often V1 |
| **Dental / sinus causes** | Odontogenic abscess, sinusitis | Continuous pain, tenderness, fever, fails carbamazepine |

**Highlight:** Most common cause is neurovascular compression (idiopathic classic trigeminal neuralgia). Always rule out multiple sclerosis if patient <40 or bilateral.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria (International Headache Society, ICHD-3):**

- A: At least three attacks of unilateral facial pain in trigeminal distribution.
- B: Pain has all of: (1) paroxysmal, lasting seconds to <2 min; (2) severe intensity; (3) electric shock / stabbing / sharp quality; (4) precipitated by innocuous stimuli (e.g., touch, chewing, cold wind).
- C: Not better accounted for by another diagnosis.
- **Key:** No diagnostic test *confirms* the diagnosis – it is clinical. MRI is to exclude secondary causes.

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| **MRI brain with contrast (including high-resolution CISS sequence)** | Visualises trigeminal nerve, cerebellopontine angle, brainstem | Rule out tumour, MS plaque, vascular loop compression | Normal: idiopathic TN. Abnormal: look for enhancing plaque (MS), mass, or artery indenting nerve root |
| **MRA (time-of-flight)** | Arterial loops (e.g., SCA) compressing nerve | If MRI suspicious for neurovascular conflict; surgical planning | Direct contact = supports classic TN; no contact = does not rule out |
| **Trigeminal reflex testing (blink reflex) – rarely needed** | Delayed R1 and R2 components | If atypical features; objective measurement of trigeminal dysfunction | Prolonged or absent response suggests demyelination or compression |
| **Blood tests (ESR, ACE, ANA, Lyme serology)** | Inflammatory or infectious markers | If atypical (young age, bilateral, constant pain) | Elevated ESR/CRP: consider giant cell arteritis if V1. ACE: sarcoid. ANA: vasculitis |

**Pattern Recognition:**  
- A single finding that confirms classical TN? **No single test.** Diagnosis is clinical.  
- A single finding that rules out TN? **Sensory loss on exam.** If you find pinprick or touch numbness: think secondary cause (MS, tumour). Classic TN should have normal sensation.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (First Hour – ED setting):**

- **Acute pain crisis:**  
  - IV lidocaine 1–2 mg/kg over 10 min (monitor BP, ECG) – reduces nerve hyperexcitability.  
  - Or IV fosphenytoin 15–20 mg PE/kg loading dose – same rationale.  
  - Avoid opiates – they’re ineffective, cause sedation, and miss the neuralgic mechanism.

**Why not opioids?** Neuropathic pain is not opioid-responsive. Opiates dull the brain, not the nerve.

**Ward (24–72 hours – Start/maintain therapy):**

| Drug | Dose / Route | Why It Works | Notes |
|------|--------------|--------------|-------|
| **Carbamazepine** (first-line) | 100–200 mg PO BID, titrate up to 400–800 mg/day until pain free or side effects | Blocks sodium channels, suppresses ephaptic transmission | Watch for hyponatremia, ataxia, rash (Stevens-Johnson if HLA-B*1502 positive – screen in Asian patients) |
| **Oxcarbazepine** (alternative) | 300–600 mg PO BID, max 1800 mg/day | Similar mechanism, fewer drug interactions, less hyponatremia | Better tolerated; can switch if carbamazepine fails |
| **Baclofen** (adjunct) | 5–10 mg PO TID, max 80 mg/day | GABA-B agonist – increases presynaptic inhibition | Useful if suboptimal response to carbamazepine; can cause sedation |
| **Lamotrigine** (second-line if not tolerated) | 25 mg daily, slow dose escalation to 200–400 mg/day | Stabilises neuronal membranes | Risk of Stevens-Johnson if dose escalated too fast |

**Long-Term (Outpatient Refinement):**

- If adequate response: continue carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine; monitor blood levels (therapeutic: 6–12 µg/mL carbamazepine).
- If refractory (despite two drugs at adequate dose): consider **neurosurgical intervention**.

**Surgical options (for specialist referral):**

| Procedure | Mechanism | Efficacy | Notes |
|-----------|-----------|----------|-------|
| **Microvascular decompression (MVD)** | Open craniotomy – separate artery from nerve | 80–90% pain-free at 10 years | Best for classic TN; requires no sensory loss; higher risk (CSF leak, cranial nerve palsy) |
| **Gamma knife radiosurgery** | Focused radiation – induces demyelination at nerve root | 70–80% pain-free at 5 years | Less invasive; delayed effect (weeks to months); can cause facial numbness |
| **Percutaneous rhizotomy / balloon compression** | Chemical or mechanical nerve damage | 60–80%; high recurrence | Causes facial numbness; used in elderly or frail patients |

**What NOT to do (Common Pitfalls):**

- Do not prescribe carbamazepine without checking CBC (risk of agranulocytosis) and LFTs.
- Do not use carbamazepine in patients with known pregnancy – it’s teratogenic.
- Do not delay MRI – missing an MS plaque or tumour is catastrophic.
- Do not dismiss dental pain – always examine intraorally and request dental OPG if in doubt.
- Do not give opiates as monotherapy – they will not work; they may mask progression.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Trigeminal Neuralgia | Postherpetic Neuralgia (V1) | Cluster Headache | Temporomandibular Joint Disorder (TMD) | Dental Abscess |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------------|------------------|----------------------------------------|----------------|
| **Pain quality** | Electric shock, stabbing, paroxysmal | Burning, aching, allodynia, constant + paroxysms | Sharp, boring, stabbing; severe; constant during attack | Dull ache, pressure, sometimes sharp on movement | Throbbing, continuous, worse with heat |
| **Location** | Unilateral, V2/V3 > V1 | Unilateral, typically V1 (forehead, eye) | Unilateral, orbital / temporal, often involves autonomic | Cheek, jaw, temple, ear | Localised to one tooth / gum |
| **Time course** | Seconds to <2 min | Constant background; paroxysms ongoing | 15–180 min, daily for weeks, then remission | Chronic, worse with chewing | Hours to days; progressive |
| **Triggers** | Touch, chewing, talking, cold wind | None/minimal; touch may worsen | Alcohol, histamine, nitroglycerin; not light touch | Chewing, clenching, yawning | Hot/cold, biting |
| **Autonomic symptoms** | None | None (unless concurrent Horner’s from zoster) | Ipsilateral lacrimation, rhinorrhoea, miosis, ptosis | None | None |
| **Neurological exam** | Normal (sensation intact) | Hypoaesthesia, allodynia, potential corneal reflex loss | Normal (may have transient Horner’s) | Normal | Normal |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Response to carbamazepine | History of herpes zoster rash in same distribution | Clockwork periodicity; restlessness | Tenderness over TMJ, clicking/crepitus | Foul taste, fever, trismus, tooth tenderness |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What To Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Chronic pain / disability** | Patient stops eating, loses weight, becomes depressed, isolates | Malnutrition, depression, suicidal ideation (10–20% of refractory TN) | Pain team referral; psychological support; escalate to MVD |
| **Suicide** | Rare but documented – prolonged untreated severe pain | Expressing hopelessness, giving away belongings, saying goodbye | Emergency psychiatric assessment; hospital admission; rapid pain control |
| **Drug toxicity** | Carbamazepine-induced hyponatremia (<125 mmol/L) | Confusion, falls, seizures | Check sodium; stop or reduce carbamazepine; replace with oxcarbazepine |
| **Oversedation / ataxia** | Falls, especially in elderly on high-dose carbamazepine | Gait instability, nystagmus, slurred speech | Reduce dose; check levels; switch to low-dose lamotrigine |
| **Misdiagnosis leading to unnecessary surgery** | Dental extraction, root canal, TMJ surgery – pain persists | Multiple dental operations, no relief | Recognise the pattern; start carbamazepine as diagnostic trial |
| **Missed secondary cause** | Underlying MS or tumour progresses | New focal deficits (e.g., trigeminal sensory loss, diplopia, hearing loss) | Urgent MRI; neurology/neurosurgery consult |

**Late Presentation Patterns (Exam Stems):**

- *“A 35-year-old woman with bilateral trigeminal neuralgia and recent optic neuritis.”* → MS until proven otherwise.
- *“A 70-year-old man with trigeminal neuralgia who develops ataxia and deafness after 3 years.”* → Acoustic neuroma (missed initially).
- *“A 50-year-old diabetic with V1 trigeminal neuralgia and a resolving vesicular rash.”* → Postherpetic neuralgia; treat with gabapentin or amitriptyline, not carbamazepine.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic – “TRIGGER”:**

- **T** – Trigger zones (touch, talk, toothbrushing)  
- **R** – Refractory period after each paroxysm (seconds to minutes)  
- **I** – Ice pick / electric shock quality  
- **G** – Glabellar area (V1) or gum (V2/V3) – know the divisions  
- **G** – Gabapentin? No! Carbamazepine is first-line  
- **E** – Exclude MS and tumour with MRI  
- **R** – Response to carbamazepine – diagnostic and therapeutic  

**Mnemonic – “BEST” for secondary causes:**

- **B** – Brainstem signs (MS, tumour)  
- **E** – Extra-trigeminal symptoms (hearing loss, vertigo, headache)  
- **S** – Sensory loss on exam  
- **T** – Twenties (age <40) or bilateral  

**Clinical Pearls:**

1. **Carbamazepine is both diagnostic and therapeutic:** A good response to a low dose (200 mg/day) strongly supports TN. Failure suggests a different pain mechanism.
2. **Examine the pinprick of the face:** If you find numbness in any trigeminal division, do not label as classic TN – order MRI.
3. **Never trust a dental diagnosis unless you have examined the mouth:** Many TN patients are sent for unnecessary dental procedures. A simple tap on the teeth with a tongue depressor will tell you if it’s dental (tender) or not.
4. **Watch for hyponatremia in the elderly** on carbamazepine – check sodium at 2 weeks and then monthly for 3 months.
5. **In MS-related TN, carbamazepine still works** but may need higher doses; consider add-on lamotrigine.
6. **Surgical treatment should not be delayed for years** – early referral to a specialist centre can prevent disability and depression.
7. **Always ask about a history of shingles** – especially in V1 pain; postherpetic neuralgia requires different management (gabapentin, lidocaine patch, tricyclics).

**Common Mistakes to Avoid:**

- Giving opiates for acute pain – they are ineffective and delay correct diagnosis.
- Starting carbamazepine without checking CBC, LFTs, and HLA-B*1502 in at-risk ethnicities (Han Chinese, Thai, Malay).
- Forgetting that bilateral TN is NOT classic – always scan.
- Assuming a patient with facial pain and a recent dental extraction has post-extraction neuralgia – if the pain has the same paroxysmal quality, treat as TN.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / neurology / neurosurgery:**

- **Any sensory loss** on examination → urgent MRI (exclude mass or MS) → refer to neurology.
- **Bilateral symptoms** → same as above.
- **Refractory pain** despite adequate doses of two anticonvulsants (carbamazepine + oxcarbazepine or lamotrigine) → discuss with neurology for surgical referral.
- **New focal neurological signs** (ataxia, hearing loss, diplopia, facial weakness) → immediate neurosurgery or neurology consult; MRI today.

**When to call critical care (ICU / crash team):**

- **Seizures** (from carbamazepine toxicity or hyponatremia <120) → stop drug; check sodium; call medical registrar / ICU for severe hyponatremia correction (risk of osmotic demyelination).
- **Suicidal ideation** with active plan → psychiatry emergency; consider 1:1 nursing; may need ICU for safety if agitated.
- **Stevens-Johnson syndrome** (fever, rash, mucosal ulcers) → stop all suspect drugs; call dermatology; ICU if epidermal detachment >10% BSA.

**Specific escalation thresholds:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| NEWS2 | ≥5 | Escalate to registrar; consider sepsis if febrile (rare in TN; check for CNS infection if on carbamazepine) |
| GCS | ≤14 | Stop carbamazepine; check drug level; sodium; CT head if focal signs |
| Lactate | >2 mmol/L | Unexplained – consider bowel ischaemia? (rare) or sepsis from aspiration? (patient avoids eating) |
| Sodium | <125 mmol/L | Stop carbamazepine; fluid restrict; call medical registrar |
| Carbamazepine level | >12 µg/mL | Reduce or stop; check ECG (QT interval) |

**Remember:** Most TN patients are managed in primary care. If they become unwell with confusion, falls, or uncontrolled pain, admit and follow above. The goal is to give them a life without fear of the next shock.

rawMarkdown:
## Trigeminal Neuralgia
**One-liner:** Paroxysmal, severe, unilateral facial pain in CN V distribution; often idiopathic or due to vascular compression; can be debilitating and misdiagnosed as dental pain.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**  
A 68-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 6-week history of “electric shock” pains in her right cheek and jaw. She winces when speaking and avoids touching her face. The pain comes in brief bursts lasting seconds, occurring up to 20 times per day. She saw her dentist twice, had a root canal, and even an extraction – but the pain persists. She can’t brush her teeth or shave. Neurological exam is normal.

**Pattern Clues Dissected:**

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Unilateral pain in cheek/jaw | Trigeminal nerve (V2/V3) distribution |
| Electric shock / stabbing quality | Neuralgic pain, not nociceptive |
| Brief paroxysms (seconds) | Characteristic of trigeminal neuralgia (TN) |
| Triggered by light touch, chewing, talking | Abnormally hypersensitive trigeminovascular system |
| Normal neurological exam | No sensory loss / motor weakness (if present, consider mass lesion or multiple sclerosis) |
| Age >50 | Classic idiopathic TN peaks in 50s–70s |
| Multiple dental procedures with no relief | Classic “red flag” – always ask about dental history |
| Avoids face-touching / uses hand to protect | “Trigger zone” phenomenon – patient behaviour gives it away |

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

1. **Pain in the first division (V1) only** – present as retro-orbital or forehead pain. May mimic cluster headache or sinusitis. Clue: pain is electric, not pulsatile, and involves the eye.
2. **Constant dull ache between paroxysms** – seen in refractory TN or when neurovascular conflict is severe. Can be confused with trigeminal neuropathy.
3. **Bilateral pain** – very rare in classic TN; think MS or brainstem lesion. Always examine for other neurological signs.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** Trigeminal neuralgia is a disorder of the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V) characterised by sudden, severe, brief, recurrent episodes of electric shock–like pain in the face. It’s like having a short-circuit in the nerve – a harmless touch becomes “loaded” and fires a pain signal.

**Why it matters clinically:**  
- Frequency: ~4–13 per 100,000; more common in women and in the elderly.  
- Stakes: Misdiagnosis leads to unnecessary dental extractions, root canals, or temporomandibular joint (TMJ) surgery.  
- Common pitfalls: Delayed treatment (suffering for months); missing secondary causes like multiple sclerosis (MS) or posterior fossa tumour.  
- Good news: Highly treatable – carbamazepine is effective in 70–80%. Bad news: refractory cases may require neurosurgery.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The Story:**  
The trigeminal nerve exits the brainstem and passes through the cerebellopontine angle. In most cases of trigeminal neuralgia, a blood vessel (usually the superior cerebellar artery) loops and compresses the nerve at the root entry zone. This causes demyelination of the nerve’s myelin sheath – the insulation that normally keeps signals fast and private. Once demyelinated, the nerve becomes hyperexcitable. Neighbouring axons cross-talk (ephaptic transmission) and a single light touch on the face can trigger a massive burst of impulses that the brain interprets as excruciating pain.

**Why each key sign happens:**

| Sign | Pathophys mechanism |
|------|---------------------|
| Electric shock quality | Synchronous high-frequency firing of demyelinated axons |
| Brief paroxysms | After a burst, the nerve is briefly refractory – then ready again |
| Trigger zones (cheek, lip, gum) | Demyelinated areas have lowered threshold – touch is enough to depolarise them |
| Unilateral | Lesion is on one side of the nerve (often vascular loop) |
| Normal motor/sensory exam | Demyelination is in sensory axons; motor function (muscles of mastication) is spared unless the motor root is affected (rare; then think mass lesion) |
| No autonomic symptoms | Unlike cluster headache – no tearing, rhinorrhea, miosis |
| Progressive frequency | Over time, demyelination worsens; ephaptic transmission becomes easier |

**Connection to pattern recognition:** The patient who avoids touching her face is protecting the trigger zones. The dental history reveals misdirected treatment – a key pointer that the pain is neuropathic, not odontogenic.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Cause Category | Example / Specific Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic / Classic** | Neurovascular compression (most common) | Age >50; no other neurological signs; excellent response to anticonvulsants |
| **Secondary – Demyelination** | Multiple sclerosis | Bilateral TN; young adult; prior optic neuritis or other MS symptoms; brainstem signs on exam |
| **Secondary – Mass lesion** | Acoustic neuroma, meningioma, epidermoid cyst | Headache, hearing loss, ataxia, trigeminal sensory loss, cranial neuropathies (e.g., CN VII, VIII) |
| **Secondary – Vascular** | Arteriovenous malformation, aneurysm (rare) | Pulsatile tinnitus; focal deficit; may be younger |
| **Secondary – Inflammatory** | Sarcoidosis, vasculitis | Multisystem involvement (lungs, skin, eyes); raised ACE |
| **Postherpetic neuralgia** | Herpes zoster affecting CN V | Vesicular rash before pain; pain is constant + paroxysmal; often V1 |
| **Dental / sinus causes** | Odontogenic abscess, sinusitis | Continuous pain, tenderness, fever, fails carbamazepine |

**Highlight:** Most common cause is neurovascular compression (idiopathic classic trigeminal neuralgia). Always rule out multiple sclerosis if patient <40 or bilateral.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Criteria (International Headache Society, ICHD-3):**

- A: At least three attacks of unilateral facial pain in trigeminal distribution.
- B: Pain has all of: (1) paroxysmal, lasting seconds to <2 min; (2) severe intensity; (3) electric shock / stabbing / sharp quality; (4) precipitated by innocuous stimuli (e.g., touch, chewing, cold wind).
- C: Not better accounted for by another diagnosis.
- **Key:** No diagnostic test *confirms* the diagnosis – it is clinical. MRI is to exclude secondary causes.

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| **MRI brain with contrast (including high-resolution CISS sequence)** | Visualises trigeminal nerve, cerebellopontine angle, brainstem | Rule out tumour, MS plaque, vascular loop compression | Normal: idiopathic TN. Abnormal: look for enhancing plaque (MS), mass, or artery indenting nerve root |
| **MRA (time-of-flight)** | Arterial loops (e.g., SCA) compressing nerve | If MRI suspicious for neurovascular conflict; surgical planning | Direct contact = supports classic TN; no contact = does not rule out |
| **Trigeminal reflex testing (blink reflex) – rarely needed** | Delayed R1 and R2 components | If atypical features; objective measurement of trigeminal dysfunction | Prolonged or absent response suggests demyelination or compression |
| **Blood tests (ESR, ACE, ANA, Lyme serology)** | Inflammatory or infectious markers | If atypical (young age, bilateral, constant pain) | Elevated ESR/CRP: consider giant cell arteritis if V1. ACE: sarcoid. ANA: vasculitis |

**Pattern Recognition:**  
- A single finding that confirms classical TN? **No single test.** Diagnosis is clinical.  
- A single finding that rules out TN? **Sensory loss on exam.** If you find pinprick or touch numbness: think secondary cause (MS, tumour). Classic TN should have normal sensation.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (First Hour – ED setting):**

- **Acute pain crisis:**  
  - IV lidocaine 1–2 mg/kg over 10 min (monitor BP, ECG) – reduces nerve hyperexcitability.  
  - Or IV fosphenytoin 15–20 mg PE/kg loading dose – same rationale.  
  - Avoid opiates – they’re ineffective, cause sedation, and miss the neuralgic mechanism.

**Why not opioids?** Neuropathic pain is not opioid-responsive. Opiates dull the brain, not the nerve.

**Ward (24–72 hours – Start/maintain therapy):**

| Drug | Dose / Route | Why It Works | Notes |
|------|--------------|--------------|-------|
| **Carbamazepine** (first-line) | 100–200 mg PO BID, titrate up to 400–800 mg/day until pain free or side effects | Blocks sodium channels, suppresses ephaptic transmission | Watch for hyponatremia, ataxia, rash (Stevens-Johnson if HLA-B*1502 positive – screen in Asian patients) |
| **Oxcarbazepine** (alternative) | 300–600 mg PO BID, max 1800 mg/day | Similar mechanism, fewer drug interactions, less hyponatremia | Better tolerated; can switch if carbamazepine fails |
| **Baclofen** (adjunct) | 5–10 mg PO TID, max 80 mg/day | GABA-B agonist – increases presynaptic inhibition | Useful if suboptimal response to carbamazepine; can cause sedation |
| **Lamotrigine** (second-line if not tolerated) | 25 mg daily, slow dose escalation to 200–400 mg/day | Stabilises neuronal membranes | Risk of Stevens-Johnson if dose escalated too fast |

**Long-Term (Outpatient Refinement):**

- If adequate response: continue carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine; monitor blood levels (therapeutic: 6–12 µg/mL carbamazepine).
- If refractory (despite two drugs at adequate dose): consider **neurosurgical intervention**.

**Surgical options (for specialist referral):**

| Procedure | Mechanism | Efficacy | Notes |
|-----------|-----------|----------|-------|
| **Microvascular decompression (MVD)** | Open craniotomy – separate artery from nerve | 80–90% pain-free at 10 years | Best for classic TN; requires no sensory loss; higher risk (CSF leak, cranial nerve palsy) |
| **Gamma knife radiosurgery** | Focused radiation – induces demyelination at nerve root | 70–80% pain-free at 5 years | Less invasive; delayed effect (weeks to months); can cause facial numbness |
| **Percutaneous rhizotomy / balloon compression** | Chemical or mechanical nerve damage | 60–80%; high recurrence | Causes facial numbness; used in elderly or frail patients |

**What NOT to do (Common Pitfalls):**

- Do not prescribe carbamazepine without checking CBC (risk of agranulocytosis) and LFTs.
- Do not use carbamazepine in patients with known pregnancy – it’s teratogenic.
- Do not delay MRI – missing an MS plaque or tumour is catastrophic.
- Do not dismiss dental pain – always examine intraorally and request dental OPG if in doubt.
- Do not give opiates as monotherapy – they will not work; they may mask progression.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Trigeminal Neuralgia | Postherpetic Neuralgia (V1) | Cluster Headache | Temporomandibular Joint Disorder (TMD) | Dental Abscess |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------------|------------------|----------------------------------------|----------------|
| **Pain quality** | Electric shock, stabbing, paroxysmal | Burning, aching, allodynia, constant + paroxysms | Sharp, boring, stabbing; severe; constant during attack | Dull ache, pressure, sometimes sharp on movement | Throbbing, continuous, worse with heat |
| **Location** | Unilateral, V2/V3 > V1 | Unilateral, typically V1 (forehead, eye) | Unilateral, orbital / temporal, often involves autonomic | Cheek, jaw, temple, ear | Localised to one tooth / gum |
| **Time course** | Seconds to <2 min | Constant background; paroxysms ongoing | 15–180 min, daily for weeks, then remission | Chronic, worse with chewing | Hours to days; progressive |
| **Triggers** | Touch, chewing, talking, cold wind | None/minimal; touch may worsen | Alcohol, histamine, nitroglycerin; not light touch | Chewing, clenching, yawning | Hot/cold, biting |
| **Autonomic symptoms** | None | None (unless concurrent Horner’s from zoster) | Ipsilateral lacrimation, rhinorrhoea, miosis, ptosis | None | None |
| **Neurological exam** | Normal (sensation intact) | Hypoaesthesia, allodynia, potential corneal reflex loss | Normal (may have transient Horner’s) | Normal | Normal |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Response to carbamazepine | History of herpes zoster rash in same distribution | Clockwork periodicity; restlessness | Tenderness over TMJ, clicking/crepitus | Foul taste, fever, trismus, tooth tenderness |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What To Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Chronic pain / disability** | Patient stops eating, loses weight, becomes depressed, isolates | Malnutrition, depression, suicidal ideation (10–20% of refractory TN) | Pain team referral; psychological support; escalate to MVD |
| **Suicide** | Rare but documented – prolonged untreated severe pain | Expressing hopelessness, giving away belongings, saying goodbye | Emergency psychiatric assessment; hospital admission; rapid pain control |
| **Drug toxicity** | Carbamazepine-induced hyponatremia (<125 mmol/L) | Confusion, falls, seizures | Check sodium; stop or reduce carbamazepine; replace with oxcarbazepine |
| **Oversedation / ataxia** | Falls, especially in elderly on high-dose carbamazepine | Gait instability, nystagmus, slurred speech | Reduce dose; check levels; switch to low-dose lamotrigine |
| **Misdiagnosis leading to unnecessary surgery** | Dental extraction, root canal, TMJ surgery – pain persists | Multiple dental operations, no relief | Recognise the pattern; start carbamazepine as diagnostic trial |
| **Missed secondary cause** | Underlying MS or tumour progresses | New focal deficits (e.g., trigeminal sensory loss, diplopia, hearing loss) | Urgent MRI; neurology/neurosurgery consult |

**Late Presentation Patterns (Exam Stems):**

- *“A 35-year-old woman with bilateral trigeminal neuralgia and recent optic neuritis.”* → MS until proven otherwise.
- *“A 70-year-old man with trigeminal neuralgia who develops ataxia and deafness after 3 years.”* → Acoustic neuroma (missed initially).
- *“A 50-year-old diabetic with V1 trigeminal neuralgia and a resolving vesicular rash.”* → Postherpetic neuralgia; treat with gabapentin or amitriptyline, not carbamazepine.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic – “TRIGGER”:**

- **T** – Trigger zones (touch, talk, toothbrushing)  
- **R** – Refractory period after each paroxysm (seconds to minutes)  
- **I** – Ice pick / electric shock quality  
- **G** – Glabellar area (V1) or gum (V2/V3) – know the divisions  
- **G** – Gabapentin? No! Carbamazepine is first-line  
- **E** – Exclude MS and tumour with MRI  
- **R** – Response to carbamazepine – diagnostic and therapeutic  

**Mnemonic – “BEST” for secondary causes:**

- **B** – Brainstem signs (MS, tumour)  
- **E** – Extra-trigeminal symptoms (hearing loss, vertigo, headache)  
- **S** – Sensory loss on exam  
- **T** – Twenties (age <40) or bilateral  

**Clinical Pearls:**

1. **Carbamazepine is both diagnostic and therapeutic:** A good response to a low dose (200 mg/day) strongly supports TN. Failure suggests a different pain mechanism.
2. **Examine the pinprick of the face:** If you find numbness in any trigeminal division, do not label as classic TN – order MRI.
3. **Never trust a dental diagnosis unless you have examined the mouth:** Many TN patients are sent for unnecessary dental procedures. A simple tap on the teeth with a tongue depressor will tell you if it’s dental (tender) or not.
4. **Watch for hyponatremia in the elderly** on carbamazepine – check sodium at 2 weeks and then monthly for 3 months.
5. **In MS-related TN, carbamazepine still works** but may need higher doses; consider add-on lamotrigine.
6. **Surgical treatment should not be delayed for years** – early referral to a specialist centre can prevent disability and depression.
7. **Always ask about a history of shingles** – especially in V1 pain; postherpetic neuralgia requires different management (gabapentin, lidocaine patch, tricyclics).

**Common Mistakes to Avoid:**

- Giving opiates for acute pain – they are ineffective and delay correct diagnosis.
- Starting carbamazepine without checking CBC, LFTs, and HLA-B*1502 in at-risk ethnicities (Han Chinese, Thai, Malay).
- Forgetting that bilateral TN is NOT classic – always scan.
- Assuming a patient with facial pain and a recent dental extraction has post-extraction neuralgia – if the pain has the same paroxysmal quality, treat as TN.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / neurology / neurosurgery:**

- **Any sensory loss** on examination → urgent MRI (exclude mass or MS) → refer to neurology.
- **Bilateral symptoms** → same as above.
- **Refractory pain** despite adequate doses of two anticonvulsants (carbamazepine + oxcarbazepine or lamotrigine) → discuss with neurology for surgical referral.
- **New focal neurological signs** (ataxia, hearing loss, diplopia, facial weakness) → immediate neurosurgery or neurology consult; MRI today.

**When to call critical care (ICU / crash team):**

- **Seizures** (from carbamazepine toxicity or hyponatremia <120) → stop drug; check sodium; call medical registrar / ICU for severe hyponatremia correction (risk of osmotic demyelination).
- **Suicidal ideation** with active plan → psychiatry emergency; consider 1:1 nursing; may need ICU for safety if agitated.
- **Stevens-Johnson syndrome** (fever, rash, mucosal ulcers) → stop all suspect drugs; call dermatology; ICU if epidermal detachment >10% BSA.

**Specific escalation thresholds:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| NEWS2 | ≥5 | Escalate to registrar; consider sepsis if febrile (rare in TN; check for CNS infection if on carbamazepine) |
| GCS | ≤14 | Stop carbamazepine; check drug level; sodium; CT head if focal signs |
| Lactate | >2 mmol/L | Unexplained – consider bowel ischaemia? (rare) or sepsis from aspiration? (patient avoids eating) |
| Sodium | <125 mmol/L | Stop carbamazepine; fluid restrict; call medical registrar |
| Carbamazepine level | >12 µg/mL | Reduce or stop; check ECG (QT interval) |

**Remember:** Most TN patients are managed in primary care. If they become unwell with confusion, falls, or uncontrolled pain, admit and follow above. The goal is to give them a life without fear of the next shock.
